description: Module-load performance optimization for Hono, Pi SDK, and Pi CLI. Reduce fixed session overhead, IPC/RPC volume, repeated payload transfer, and bootstrap costs while keeping committed benchmark artifacts and progress tracking up to date.

userStories.title: Make the Pi CLI end-to-end benchmark produce a real workload
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the Pi CLI end-to-end scenario to actually reach model execution so it can be used as a performance signal instead of a broken placeholder.
acceptanceCriteria: The Pi CLI end-to-end benchmark reaches a real workload path and the committed artifact no longer shows `code = 0`, `mockRequests = 0`, and empty stdout/stderr with no network activity.

Running `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` produces committed `result.json`, `metrics.prom`, `ipc.ndjson`, `runner.log`, `summary.md`, and `summary.json` artifacts for the Pi CLI end-to-end scenario.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Current failing artifact: `packages/secure-exec/benchmarks/results/module-load/pi-cli-end-to-end/result.json` reports `code = 0`, `mockRequests = 0`, and no stdout/stderr. Do not treat the current Pi CLI end-to-end result as performance data until this story lands.

userStories.title: Generate machine-readable benchmark summaries and deltas
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the module-load benchmark to emit machine-readable method, byte, and timing summaries so each optimization story can track progress without manually re-analyzing raw IPC logs.
acceptanceCriteria: The committed results folder includes generated per-scenario summaries with calls by method, time by method, bytes by frame/method, iteration-level timing, and fixed session overhead fields.

The committed benchmark output includes an explicit before/after comparison against the prior baseline so later Ralph iterations can cite deltas directly from artifacts instead of manually recomputing them.
The benchmark documentation or result format makes it obvious which numbers should be copied into `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` after each story.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The current `analysis.md` is a useful one-off report, but the next wave of optimization work needs generated summaries so each iteration can record benchmark movement in `progress.txt` without hand parsing `ipc.ndjson`.

userStories.title: Add raw UDS RTT and per-session phase attribution
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need direct transport and session-phase metrics so we can distinguish socket latency, serialization overhead, and session lifecycle costs from application work.
acceptanceCriteria: The benchmark suite includes a direct UDS ping or equivalent bridge round-trip measurement for at least `1 B`, `1 KB`, and `64 KB` payloads.

The committed metrics break out session create, first execute send, execute receive, and session destroy or equivalent lifecycle phases instead of leaving the current `~109 ms` fixed cost as an unattributed bucket.
The committed results folder captures these new RTT and phase metrics alongside the existing scenario artifacts.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Today the benchmark measures full `Execute` and host-side `BridgeCall -> BridgeResponse`, but not raw UDS RTT. The analysis shows a stable `~109 ms` fixed per-session cost that needs phase attribution before we guess at a fix.

userStories.title: Add JSZip and pdf-lib scenarios to the module-load benchmark suite
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need JSZip and pdf-lib benchmark scenarios in the committed module-load performance suite so we can optimize those heavier document/archive workloads over time instead of tuning only Hono and Pi.
acceptanceCriteria: The committed module-load benchmark suite includes startup and end-to-end scenarios for `pdf-lib` and `jszip`, with committed `result.json`, `metrics.prom`, `ipc.ndjson`, `runner.log`, `summary.md`, and `summary.json` coverage for the new scenarios.
The `pdf-lib` benchmark logic is copied or adapted from `/tmp/pdfbench.js`, including a representative document workload rather than a trivial import-only sample.
The `jszip` benchmark uses a representative archive workload so committed numbers cover both module load cost and realistic zip creation or serialization work.
The committed summary and analysis outputs include cold and warm numbers for the new `pdf-lib` and `jszip` scenarios so later stories can track their movement over time.

Append the first committed baseline for the new `pdf-lib` and `jszip` scenarios to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters they exercise.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Use `/tmp/pdfbench.js` as the seed for the `pdf-lib` workload. The goal is not just to import the library, but to keep committed startup and end-to-end benchmark numbers for heavier document/archive codepaths so future perf work optimizes them too.

userStories.title: Restore repeated-session compressed JSZip benchmarking
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the JSZip end-to-end scenario to survive repeated fresh sessions with compression enabled so the benchmark can track realistic compressed archive workloads instead of the current STORE fallback.
acceptanceCriteria: The `jszip-end-to-end` scenario completes all benchmark iterations with a compressed archive path enabled instead of hanging on the third fresh session.
Committed `result.json`, `ipc.ndjson`, `summary.md`, and `summary.json` artifacts show the restored compressed JSZip scenario and preserve the current representative nested archive workload shape.
Focused regression coverage or a reproducer proves the repeated-session hang is fixed and does not regress silently.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected JSZip scenario, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: US-012 committed a stable STORE-based nested-archive baseline after repeated DEFLATE runs hung on iteration 3 inside `packages/secure-exec/benchmarks/module-load/scenario-runner.ts`.

userStories.title: Eliminate repeated `_loadPolyfill` round-trips within hot workloads
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the runtime to stop issuing thousands of identical `_loadPolyfill` RPCs during Pi startup so bridge chatter is no longer the dominant cost center.
acceptanceCriteria: Repeated `_loadPolyfill` requests inside the same hot workload are deduplicated or served from an in-runtime cache instead of crossing the bridge every time.

Pi SDK startup and Pi CLI startup show a meaningful reduction in `_loadPolyfill` call count and host bridge time versus the committed baseline, and the exact deltas are captured in committed benchmark artifacts.
The Hono startup/end-to-end control scenarios do not regress materially; if they do, the regression is explained in `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` and followed by a new story.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by caching bare-specifier polyfill probes plus package-internal resolution results inside read-only `node_modules` trees. The committed benchmark artifacts cut Pi startup `_loadPolyfill` wrapper counts roughly in half (`5240 -> 2523` for Pi SDK startup, `5247 -> 2528` for Pi CLI startup), while a direct sandbox probe now shows the remaining `_loadPolyfill` wrapper calls are unique and only `77` real non-`__bd:*` polyfill-body loads remain during Pi SDK startup. Aggregate `_loadPolyfill` method time in `comparison.md` is still confounded by unique `__bd:*` bridge-dispatch wrappers, so attribution follow-up `US-014` tracks that measurement gap explicitly.

userStories.title: Split `_loadPolyfill` benchmark attribution from `__bd:*` bridge dispatch
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the module-load benchmark to distinguish real polyfill-body loads from generic `__bd:*` bridge-dispatch wrappers so future stories can tell whether `_loadPolyfill` regressions come from repeated polyfill misses or from other bridge work.
acceptanceCriteria: Committed benchmark artifacts break the current `_loadPolyfill` bucket into at least two signals: real polyfill-body loads and generic `__bd:*` bridge-dispatch wrappers.
Pi startup scenarios expose copy-ready before/after deltas for both signals so later progress entries do not need ad hoc in-sandbox probes to understand `_loadPolyfill` changes.
Focused regression coverage or summary tests prove the new attribution stays stable across repeated benchmark reruns.
Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the new `_loadPolyfill` attribution fields.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by adding `_loadPolyfill` target attribution to IPC observability plus a benchmark-summary fallback classifier for older logs. The committed `summary.md` / `comparison.md` artifacts now expose copy-ready `real polyfill-body loads` vs `__bd:* bridge-dispatch wrappers` deltas for Pi startup scenarios.

userStories.title: Shrink `_loadPolyfill` payload transfer with identifier or hash caching
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need repeated polyfill payloads to travel once and then be referenced cheaply so Pi workloads stop paying multi-megabyte response costs.
acceptanceCriteria: Repeated polyfill source payloads are served by identifier, hash, manifest, or an equivalent cache-friendly protocol instead of sending the full body every time.
Pi SDK startup and Pi CLI startup show a meaningful reduction in BridgeResponse bytes and warm wall time versus baseline, and the committed artifacts make that byte reduction visible.
The new caching scheme remains correct across warm sessions, fresh sessions, and benchmark re-runs; there is regression coverage for cache hits and misses.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by extending the existing `_loadPolyfill` cache-envelope path to include dispatch-backed source loads (`__bd:_loadFile:*`, `__bd:_loadFileSync:*`) while still excluding other `__bd:*` results that can encode session-local state. The committed Pi startup artifacts cut `send:BridgeResponse` by `~4.02-4.03 MB` per iteration (`-53.78%`) and reduced warm wall time by `31.51%` for Pi SDK startup and `21.34%` for Pi CLI startup. Focused cache hit/miss regression coverage lives in `packages/v8/test/context-snapshot-behavior.test.ts`. Follow-up `US-015` tracks a separate full-suite `jszip-end-to-end` hang discovered during verification.

userStories.title: Restore full-suite `jszip-end-to-end` benchmark completion
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` to finish through `jszip-end-to-end` again so later performance stories can rerun the full committed suite without falling back to ad hoc targeted scenarios.
acceptanceCriteria: Running `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` completes through `jszip-end-to-end` without hanging or manual intervention.
A focused reproducer or regression test proves the failure no longer appears when `jszip-end-to-end` runs after earlier scenarios in the full suite.

Committed `packages/secure-exec/benchmarks/results/module-load/jszip-end-to-end/*` artifacts stay valid for the restored full-suite path, and any benchmark movement is appended to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: While verifying US-005, the full `bench:module-load` flow still stalled when it reached `jszip-end-to-end`. A direct A/B check with the new `_loadPolyfill` cache gate locally disabled still timed out, so this blocker is tracked separately from payload-caching work.

userStories.title: Stop resending large bridge bootstrap payloads on every execute
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need repeated executes to avoid retransmitting large static bootstrap payloads so the steady-state module-load path is not dominated by repeated setup bytes.
acceptanceCriteria: Repeated executions do not resend the same large bridge/bootstrap payload in full when the content is unchanged, or the benchmark proves a smaller equivalent design.
Committed benchmark artifacts expose before/after `Execute` frame size so the byte movement from this story is obvious.
Affected scenarios show a meaningful reduction in steady-state wall time or transport bytes versus baseline without breaking correctness.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by adding cross-session bridge snapshot refs to `Execute` frames, backed by native snapshot-ref caching plus a retry-on-ref-miss fallback. The committed benchmark artifacts cut average `send:Execute` bytes by `697699` bytes/iteration across the suite (for example `1243904 -> 546205` on Pi SDK startup and `1244082 -> 546383` on Pi CLI startup), while a focused Hono repro now shows warm fresh-session `Execute` frames at `197216` bytes with `bridgeCodeBytes = 0` and `bridgeCodeRefBytes = 71`. Follow-up `US-016` tracks the remaining static `postRestoreScript` bootstrap bytes that still travel on every warm execute.

userStories.title: Deduplicate static `postRestoreScript` bootstrap bytes in warm execute frames
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the remaining static post-restore bootstrap script to stop travelling on every warm execute so the steady-state `Execute` frame shrinks beyond the new bridge snapshot-ref floor.
acceptanceCriteria: Warm fresh-session `Execute` frames no longer resend the same large static `postRestoreScript` bootstrap body in full when the content is unchanged, or the benchmark proves a smaller equivalent design.
Committed benchmark artifacts expose before/after `send:Execute` bytes for at least the Hono control and one Pi scenario, and the byte movement is obvious in `comparison.md`.
Focused regression coverage proves the refactored post-restore bootstrap still applies console/require/dynamic-import setup correctly after bridge replacement.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: US-006 removed the repeated `bridgeCode` transfer on warm fresh sessions, but warm `Execute` frames still carry about `196 KB` of static `postRestoreScript` bootstrap on every iteration.

userStories.title: Cache module resolution, package metadata, and filesystem probes
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need resolver and file existence checks on the hot startup path to be memoized so Pi workloads stop repeating the same expensive lookup work.
acceptanceCriteria: Positive and negative module resolution results, package metadata reads, and repeated filesystem probe results on the hot load path are memoized or otherwise deduplicated safely.
Pi startup scenarios show lower `_resolveModule` and `_fsExists` counts and host bridge time versus baseline, with exact before/after values captured in committed benchmark artifacts.
Regression coverage proves the new caching does not break module graph correctness, path canonicalization, or permission enforcement.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by sharing resolver caches across require/createRequire/dynamic-import helpers, fixing sync resolution for projected package paths and dotted package directories, and caching/invalidation for repeated `fs.existsSync` probes. `_resolveModule` fell out of the Pi bridge-method tables, while `_fsExists` dropped to `32` calls in Pi SDK end-to-end, `41` in Pi CLI startup, and `43` in Pi CLI end-to-end. Remaining startup byte-transfer cost maps to the existing `_loadPolyfill` / preload follow-up work in `US-008`.

userStories.title: Preload or snapshot the hottest bootstrap assets
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the hottest polyfills and bootstrap assets to arrive via snapshot or a preloaded bundle so the runtime does not reconstruct the same startup surface through bridge calls every run.
acceptanceCriteria: The hottest bootstrap assets are delivered through snapshot or another preloaded no-RPC path where it is actually cheaper than the current bridge-driven load behavior.
Committed benchmark artifacts compare the old and new bootstrap path, including any warm snapshot tradeoff, and record the effect on Pi startup/end-to-end scenarios.
The optimization keeps correctness for both simple Hono controls and Pi workloads, with focused regression coverage on whichever bootstrap surface changed.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Completed by snapshot-preloading the safe Pi builtin set (`util`, `buffer`, `string_decoder`, `events`, `path`, `internal/mime`, `constants`, `vm`, `tty`) into the bridge bootstrap, teaching `require-setup` to satisfy those bare requires from the warmed snapshot bundle before `_loadPolyfill`, and extending `WarmSnapshot` to register the bridge-code ref immediately so the first fresh-session `Execute` can reuse the warmed snapshot path. The committed benchmark artifacts cut Pi `send:Execute` bytes from about `507-508 KB` to `13-14 KB` per warm iteration, while focused runtime regression proves the configured preloaded builtins avoid `_loadPolyfill`. Follow-up `US-017` tracks the larger builtin modules that still fail under snapshot-preload and keep the remaining `_loadPolyfill` response bytes on the hot path.

userStories.title: Stabilize broader builtin snapshot-preload coverage for Pi startup
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the remaining hot builtin modules on Pi startup to preload safely through the snapshot path so the bridge no longer pays large `_loadPolyfill` response bytes for modules that still cannot be snapshotted today.
acceptanceCriteria: The next hot builtin candidates on the Pi startup path (`stream`, `stream/web`, `crypto`, `zlib`, `assert`, `url`, or an updated subset backed by committed evidence) either load correctly from the snapshot-preload path or are explicitly ruled out with a concrete failure reason.
Committed benchmark artifacts show whether expanding the preload set reduces Pi `_loadPolyfill` bridge time or BridgeResponse bytes beyond the current `send:Execute` win from `US-008`.
Focused regression coverage protects snapshot restore and require-time correctness for any newly preloaded builtins.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: US-008 landed the safe preload set, but broader builtin candidates such as `stream`, `stream/web`, `crypto`, `zlib`, `assert`, and `url` still destabilize snapshot restore or fail at require time when forced onto the snapshot-preload path.

userStories.title: Bypass `_loadPolyfill` for hot `__bd:*` bridge-dispatch wrappers
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the hottest remaining `__bd:*` bridge-dispatch wrappers to use a direct bridge route instead of the string-encoded `_loadPolyfill.applySyncPromise()` path so Pi startup is no longer dominated by wrapper dispatch overhead.
acceptanceCriteria: The hottest remaining dispatch-backed bridge paths currently emitted as `__bd:*` wrapper loads use a dedicated bridge route (native bridge globals, binary dispatch opcodes, or an equivalent non-`_loadPolyfill` path) instead of the current `"__bd:" + method + ":" + JSON.stringify(args)` `_loadPolyfill.applySyncPromise()` shim.
Committed benchmark artifacts show a meaningful reduction in `__bd:* bridge-dispatch wrappers` time and/or response bytes for Pi SDK startup and Pi CLI startup versus the current baseline.
Focused regression coverage proves moved handlers preserve current behavior for undefined arguments, structured error fields (`name`, `code`, `stack`), and missing-handler fallback semantics.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the `real polyfill-body loads` vs `__bd:* bridge-dispatch wrappers` splits.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Current committed Pi startup baselines still spend most `_loadPolyfill` time in `__bd:*` wrappers rather than real polyfill bodies: Pi SDK startup shows `2437` wrapper calls / `741.039 ms` / `2735956` response bytes per iteration, and Pi CLI startup shows `2440` wrapper calls / `848.487 ms` / `2736128` response bytes per iteration. The current implementation path is built in `packages/nodejs/src/execution-driver.ts` `buildBridgeDispatchShim()` and decoded in `packages/nodejs/src/bridge-handlers.ts` `buildModuleLoadingBridgeHandlers()`.

userStories.title: Preload or manifest-cache hot projected package source files
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the hottest read-only package source files under projected `node_modules` to load from a snapshot or manifest cache instead of repeated `__bd:_loadFileSync` source transfers so Pi, pdf-lib, and JSZip stop spending bridge time on package bodies we already know are immutable.
acceptanceCriteria: Use the committed scenario summaries to identify the hottest projected package source targets on Pi startup and add a preload or manifest-cache path for an evidence-backed subset of them.

At minimum, the committed artifacts show whether the hot read-only package source loads currently sampled in the benchmark (`@mariozechner/jiti`, `@mariozechner/pi-agent-core`, `@borewit/text-codec`, or an updated data-backed subset) move off the repeated `__bd:_loadFileSync` body-transfer path on warm fresh sessions.

Committed benchmark artifacts show lower `__bd:_loadFileSync` wrapper counts or response bytes for Pi SDK startup and at least one additional scenario (`pi-cli-startup`, `pdf-lib-*`, or `jszip-*`) versus the current baseline.
Focused regression coverage proves the new cache path preserves module correctness and read-only `moduleAccess` safety for projected package files.
Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant `_loadPolyfill` target samples touched by this story.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The committed `pi-sdk-startup/summary.md` samples show the remaining wrapper traffic is mostly projected package source loads such as `__bd:_loadFileSync:[".../@borewit/text-codec/lib/index.js"]`, `__bd:_loadFileSync:[".../@mariozechner/jiti/dist/jiti.cjs"]`, and `__bd:_loadFileSync:[".../@mariozechner/pi-agent-core/dist/agent.js"]`. Builtin preload work in US-017 does not address these package-local file bodies.

userStories.title: Replace destroy Ping/Pong teardown with explicit session-destroy acknowledgment
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need normal session teardown to stop paying a connection-level Ping/Pong barrier after every `DestroySession` so low-RPC workloads like Hono are not dominated by post-execution teardown latency.
acceptanceCriteria: Normal `DestroySession` teardown no longer requires the current connection-level Ping/Pong barrier on every successful destroy, or an equivalent ordered-teardown design lands with measurably lower overhead.
Committed benchmark artifacts show a meaningful reduction in `ExecutionResult->Destroy` time and warm fixed overhead for Hono startup and at least one Pi startup scenario versus the current baseline.

IPC/runtime regression coverage proves teardown still stays ordered across normal completion, handler failure, connection close, and dispose paths without leaking sessions or dropping late stream events.
Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, including warm phase attribution fields (`ExecutionResult->Destroy`, fixed overhead) for the affected scenarios.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The current committed warm phase tables still show `ExecutionResult->Destroy` at about `102-103 ms` for Hono, pdf-lib, JSZip, Pi SDK startup, and Pi CLI startup. The current teardown path lives in `packages/v8/src/runtime.ts`, where `destroy()` sends `DestroySession` and then waits on `waitForRuntimeBarrier()` via a connection-level `Ping` / `Pong` round-trip.

userStories.title: Expose ranked bridge-target hotspots in module-load summaries
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the module-load summaries to rank the exact bridge targets still dominating `_loadPolyfill` and `__bd:*` traffic so follow-on preload and dispatch stories can target the highest-value files instead of relying on sample target snippets.
acceptanceCriteria: Per-scenario summaries and comparisons expose the top bridge targets by calls, time, and response bytes for `_loadPolyfill` traffic instead of only showing `Sample Targets`.
At minimum, the Pi startup and Pi end-to-end summaries make it obvious which projected package files or wrapper targets dominate the remaining `__bd:* bridge-dispatch wrappers` bucket.

The top-level benchmark artifacts include copy-ready target-level deltas that can be cited in `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` when deciding which files or builtins to move into US-017, US-018, or US-019.
Focused summary/derivation regression coverage proves older logs still parse correctly when target-level ranking fields are absent or partial.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The current per-scenario summaries only expose `_loadPolyfill` `Sample Targets`, which is enough to hint at hot paths like `@mariozechner/jiti`, `@mariozechner/pi-agent-core`, `@borewit/text-codec`, and `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, but not enough to rank the real time/byte winners for the remaining wrapper bucket.

userStories.title: Add isolation microbenchmarks for empty session and single hot imports
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need smaller isolation benchmarks for lifecycle-only and single-import paths so the remaining fixed-overhead, builtin-preload, wrapper-bypass, and package-cache stories can be measured without interference from full app workloads.
acceptanceCriteria: The module-load benchmark suite includes at least one lifecycle-only or empty-session scenario that exercises fresh-session creation, execute, and destroy with minimal user code.

The suite includes an import microbenchmark matrix for the highest-value remaining hotspots, covering an evidence-backed subset of hot builtins (`stream`, `stream/web`, `crypto`, `zlib`, `assert`, `url`) and at least one projected package-file target from the current Pi startup path.
Committed artifacts and summaries make it clear which microbenchmarks isolate fixed session overhead versus import/bootstrap overhead, with copy-ready numbers for `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`.
The new scenarios are stable in the full `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` suite and have focused regression coverage for their expected checks.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The current scenario catalog only covers full app-style workloads (`hono`, `pdf-lib`, `jszip`, `pi-sdk`, `pi-cli`). That is useful for end-to-end regression tracking, but it still does not isolate the remaining single-digit lifecycle floor from the builtin/package import hot spots tracked in `US-017` through `US-019`.

userStories.title: Add benchmark stability and resource-usage reporting
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the module-load benchmark to report per-scenario variance and resource usage so future perf changes can be judged on noise, CPU, and memory behavior instead of warm wall time alone.
acceptanceCriteria: Per-scenario summaries include warm-run stability fields such as min/max, median, and a spread metric (`stddev`, `MAD`, or an equivalent) for wall time and execute duration.

The benchmark artifacts capture host-runtime resource usage for each scenario, covering an evidence-backed subset such as peak RSS, CPU time, heap limit usage, or another concrete metric surfaced through the existing observability path.
Top-level summary and comparison artifacts expose these stability/resource fields in a copy-ready format for `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`.
Focused regression coverage proves the summary pipeline handles missing or partial resource metrics gracefully and keeps older artifact comparisons readable.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Current benchmark artifacts report means and targeted transport RTT percentiles, but they do not expose per-scenario wall/execute variance or any host-runtime resource metrics. That makes it harder to interpret mixed results such as Pi CLI startup cutting bridge calls roughly in half while wall time stays flat.

userStories.title: Reduce the fixed per-session overhead
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need fresh-session startup to spend less time in session lifecycle bookkeeping so even low-RPC workloads like Hono are materially faster.
acceptanceCriteria: The fixed fresh-session overhead identified in the benchmark is reduced meaningfully from the current `~109 ms` baseline, or the story produces precise attribution that isolates the remaining dominant phase.
Hono startup and Hono end-to-end show measurable warm-wall improvement versus baseline, and any Pi gains or regressions are captured in committed benchmark artifacts.
Regression coverage protects the session lifecycle or runtime bookkeeping surfaces touched by this change.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Satisfied by the committed `US-020` destroy-ack work and the refreshed benchmark artifacts: Hono warm fixed overhead is now `5.440 ms` for startup and `5.543 ms` for end-to-end, with the remaining dominant bucket attributed to `CreateSession -> InjectGlobals` plus a sub-millisecond residual rather than `ExecutionResult -> Destroy`.

userStories.title: Expand the benchmark controls for cold, warm, and host comparisons
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need the module-load benchmark to cover true cold start, same-session replay, new-session replay, host control, and warm snapshot toggles so optimizations can be attributed to the right layer.
acceptanceCriteria: The committed benchmark suite includes true cold-start timing that covers `createNodeV8Runtime()`, same-session repeated import timing, new-session repeated import timing, and a host-Node control for the same scenarios.
The benchmark can compare warm snapshot enabled vs disabled for the tracked scenarios and commit the resulting artifact set.
The committed results format makes it clear which benchmark mode is being compared so future progress entries can distinguish startup, execute, snapshot, and control-path changes.

Append before/after benchmark deltas to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` for the affected scenarios, including warm wall time, bridge calls per iteration, and the dominant IPC counters touched by this story.

If this story reveals another optimization opportunity or unresolved blocker, add it to `docs-internal/todo.md` and append a new `passes: false` follow-up story to `scripts/ralph/prd.json` before marking this story complete.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: The current benchmark excludes `createNodeV8Runtime()` from wall time and does not include a host control. Those missing controls make it too easy to confuse session cost, bootstrap cost, and actual steady-state module-load work.

userStories.title: Stabilize top-level module-load benchmark orchestration
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` to regenerate the full artifact set reliably from a clean results directory so future performance stories do not depend on manual per-scenario reruns or summary-only recovery modes.
acceptanceCriteria: The top-level `pnpm --filter secure-exec bench:module-load` flow completes across the current scenario catalog from a clean `packages/secure-exec/benchmarks/results/module-load/` directory without manual intervention.
If a recovery path is still needed, it is automatic inside the benchmark driver rather than requiring manual direct `scenario-runner.ts` reruns and ad hoc baseline copying.

Focused regression coverage or a deterministic reproduction harness protects the parent/child orchestration stall that currently appears around `pdf-lib-end-to-end` when the direct scenario runner itself succeeds.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: US-010 added `SECURE_EXEC_BENCH_REUSE_RESULTS=1` plus `SECURE_EXEC_BENCH_BASELINE_ROOT` so top-level summaries can be rebuilt from per-scenario artifacts, but the parent `run-module-load-benchmarks.ts` command still stalls intermittently around `pdf-lib-end-to-end` in this environment even though direct `scenario-runner.ts` invocations finish.

userStories.title: Restore direct Pi CLI headless execution inside standalone NodeRuntime
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I need standalone NodeRuntime headless Pi CLI execution to reach the provider path so the benchmark can drop the current CLI-module-graph plus SDK surrogate and measure the direct CLI path honestly.
acceptanceCriteria: A standalone `NodeRuntime.exec()` that imports or calls `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/main.js` or `dist/cli.js` in headless print mode reaches at least one mock Anthropic request and surfaces stdout inside the sandbox.
The `pi-cli-end-to-end` benchmark can switch from the current surrogate path back to the direct CLI headless path without regressing the committed artifact contract.
Focused regression coverage proves empty-stdin headless Pi CLI no longer exits `0` before provider traffic in standalone `NodeRuntime`.
Relevant targeted tests pass.
Touched packages typecheck.
userStories.notes: Fixed by switching the benchmark and regression harnesses to call `dist/main.js` directly inside standalone `NodeRuntime.exec()`, while forcing `process.stdin.isTTY = true` for explicit `--print` messages so Pi skips its piped-stdin probe and reaches the mock provider path.
